title: Contributing to hetiv
text: Thanks improving hetiv. package research software, helpful contributions reproducible bug reports, small regression tests, documentation fixes.

title: Contributing to hetiv
text: Install development dependencies devtools::install_deps(dependencies = TRUE). Load package devtools::load_all(). Run tests devtools::test(). Check test coverage covr::package_coverage(). Regenerate documentation roxygen changes devtools::document(). Run devtools::check() release-oriented changes. Please keep examples deterministic, offline-safe, reasonably fast. Use small simulated data tests unless larger sample needed reproduce numerical issue.

title: Contributing to hetiv
text: public API uses mathematical names Psi, Phi, SigE, H. names intentionally preserved. New internal helpers prefer clear lower-case names, focused functions, explicit error messages.

title: Contributing to hetiv
text: numerical econometric bugs, include: exact function call; dimensions input matrices; sessionInfo(); whether issue depends random seed; expected behavior observed behavior.

title: Estimating Dynamic Causal Effects with hetiv
text: hetiv provides tools measuring identifying multi-dimensional structural shocks dynamic models using two complementary IV approaches: Heteroskedasticity-IV (Rigobon 2003, Rigobon Sack 2004, Lewis 2022, Burri Kaufmann, 2026a): exploits higher variance outcome variables policy event days relative control days identify structural shocks without requiring external instruments. Proxy-IV (Mertens Ravn 2013, Stock Watson 2018): uses external instrument (proxy) correlated shock interest uncorrelated shocks. approaches implemented local projection IV estimators following Jordà (2005), directly produce impulse response functions (IRFs) across multiple horizons. Inference based heteroskedasticity-robust standard errors default. Montiel Olea et al. (2025) show standard errors suffice local-projection impulse responses weak conditions, even though multi-step forecast errors typically serially correlated. Newey-West HAC standard errors also available via cov_type = "NW" optional robustness check. package allows multiple shocks endogenous variables. Weak-instrument HAR inference implemented via generalised minimum eigenvalue test Lewis Mertens (2025), nests classical Stock-Yogo (2005) test univariate homoskedastic case. gweakivtest() direct port Matlab files Lewis Mertens (2025) available https://karelmertens.com/research/. addition, package provides kfpredict() predicting underlying unobserved shocks based Kalman filter, suggested Burri Kaufmann (2026b). vignette demonstrates functionality using simulated four-variable VAR two structural shocks.

title: Estimating Dynamic Causal Effects with hetiv
text: simulate data VAR(2) \(N = 4\) variables, \(E = 2\) event (policy) shocks, \(R = 2\) regular shocks, \(P = 2\) lags \(T = 500\) observations. model reads: \[y_t = \Psi \varepsilon_t + \Gamma v_t + \Phi(L) y_{t-1} + \beta X_t \ \ \text{} t\\mathcal{P}\] \[y_t =  \Gamma v_t + \Phi(L) y_{t-1} + \beta X_t \ \ \text{} t\\mathcal{C}\] \(\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{C}\) denote policy event days, respectively, \(\Psi\) impact matrix \(E\) policy event shocks \(\Gamma\) impact matrix \(R\) shocks. \(\Phi(L)\) conformable lag polynomial, \(X_t\) matrix deterministic terms. Regular shocks occur days. policy event occurs every 10th period (approximately 10% observations). latter introduces heteroskedasticity variance-covariance matrix reduced-form residuals hetiv() exploits identify impulse response functions. Shocks drawn standard normal distribution. impact matrix lower-triangular, corresponding identifying assumption Burri Kaufmann (2026a) first shock contemporaneous effect variables, second shock contemporaneous effect first variable. Deterministic weekday patterns added variables 3 4 illustrate role controls. event indicator Ind equals 1 policy event days 0 control days. illustrate proxy-IV approach, construct noisy external instrument two event shocks adding Gaussian noise true shocks. proxy set zero control days. mirrors typical situation high-frequency identification monetary policy shocks, high-frequency surprises recorded around policy announcement windows zero otherwise event-study sample.
code: library(hetiv)  # Dimensions N <- 4 # variables E <- 2 # event shocks R <- 2 # regular shocks P <- 2 # VAR lag order H <- 20 # IRF horizons Nevn <- 10 # Frequency of event shocks  # Impact matrix for event shocks (N x E); lower triangular for recursive ID PsiE <- matrix(0, N, E) PsiE[, 1] <- c(1.0, 0.5, 0.3, 0.2) PsiE[, 2] <- c(0.0, 1.0, -0.4, 0.3)  SigE <- 4 # event shock variance (scalar, applies to all E shocks)  # Impact matrix for regular shocks (N x R) PsiR <- matrix(0, N, R) PsiR[, 1] <- c(1.0, -0.3, 0.2, 0.1) PsiR[, 2] <- c(0.2, 1.0, -0.1, 0.4)  # VAR coefficient matrices at lags 1 and 2 Phi <- array(0, dim = c(N, N, P)) Phi[, , 1] <- matrix(c(   0.60, 0.05, -0.04, 0.03,   0.06, 0.40, 0.05, -0.03,   -0.05, 0.04, 0.50, 0.06,   0.03, -0.03, 0.05, 0.70 ), N, N, byrow = TRUE) Phi[, , 2] <- matrix(c(   0.10, 0.03, -0.02, 0.02,   0.04, 0.10, 0.03, -0.02,   -0.03, 0.02, 0.10, 0.03,   0.02, -0.02, 0.03, 0.10 ), N, N, byrow = TRUE)  # Simulate — seed is set internally by simulatedata() Nobs <- 500 Nbin <- 100 sim <- simulatedata(   Phi = Phi, SigE = SigE, PsiE = PsiE, PsiR = PsiR,   Nobs = Nobs, Nbin = Nbin, N = N, R = R, E = E,   Nevn = Nevn, P = P, eDist = 0, seed = 42 )  # Extract simulated data and event indicator y_data <- sim$y Ind <- as.integer(sim$IndE[, 1])  # Add deterministic weekday variation into variables 3 and 4 y_data[, 3] <- y_data[, 3] + 0.5 * seq_len(Nobs) %% 5 y_data[, 4] <- y_data[, 4] + 0.3 * seq_len(Nobs) %% 5 # RNG state after simulatedata(seed = 42) is deterministic, so no extra seed needed e_proxy <- sim$eE e_proxy[, 1] <- e_proxy[, 1] + rnorm(Nobs, sd = 1) e_proxy[, 2] <- e_proxy[, 2] + rnorm(Nobs, sd = 1) e_proxy[Ind == 0, ] <- 0

title: Estimating Dynamic Causal Effects with hetiv
text: First, estimate misspecified model, failing control lagged dependent variables weekday-variation. Note impose normalization impact endogenous variable every shock. value normalization can chosen norm. , choose normalization corresponds true impact matrix.
code: res_het <- hetiv(   y       = y_data,   O       = y_data,   Ind     = Ind,   P       = 0,   H       = H,   E       = E,   norm    = 1,   details = TRUE )

title: Estimating Dynamic Causal Effects with hetiv
text: Second, estimate correctly specified model, \(P = 2\) lags weekday dummies. Note weekday dummies absorb deterministic pattern added data. regressions generally include constant term. Therefore, add four weekday dummies.
code: # Weekday dummies: four indicator variables (one left out as reference) X_data <- matrix(0, nrow = Nobs, ncol = 4) for (i in 1:4) X_data[, i] <- as.integer((seq_len(Nobs)) %% 5 == (i - 1))  res_het_X <- hetiv(   y       = y_data,   O       = y_data,   X       = X_data,   Ind     = Ind,   P       = P,   H       = H,   E       = E,   norm    = 1,   details = TRUE )

title: Estimating Dynamic Causal Effects with hetiv
text: Third, use external instrument estimate model via proxy-IV. lags included second model. However, weekday dummies dropped due collinearity, event days used identify responses. Note proxy-IV function still requires Ind input, , case instrument missing, Ind = 1, can later predict unobserved shock using Kalman filter kfpredict() (see Burri Kaufmann, 2026a).
code: res_proxy <- proxyiv(   y         = y_data,   O         = y_data,   Z         = e_proxy,   Ind       = Ind,   P         = P,   H         = H,   E         = E,   norm      = 1,   recursive = FALSE,   details   = TRUE )

title: Estimating Dynamic Causal Effects with hetiv
text: proxies valid, need additional restrictions identify impulse responses. However, function allows us additionally impose zero restrictions mirroring assumptions used hetiv(). useful situation doubt validity external instruments, researcher convinced zero restriction valid.
code: res_proxy_rec <- proxyiv(   y         = y_data,   O         = y_data,   Z         = e_proxy,   Ind       = Ind,   P         = P,   H         = H,   E         = E,   norm      = 1,   recursive = TRUE,   details   = TRUE )

title: Estimating Dynamic Causal Effects with hetiv
text: estimated impact matrix \(\Psi\) gives contemporaneous responses \(N\) variables structural shock. can compare estimates across four specifications true \(\Psi\). first sight, differences small. see , still differences terms accuracy estimates, predicted shocks, strength instruments. Impact matrix estimates (\(\Psi\)) shock 1 across different specifications Impact matrix estimates (\(\Psi\)) shock 2 across different specifications
code: tab_1 <- round(data.frame(   True = PsiE[, 1],   HET_IV = res_het$Psi[, 1],   HET_IV_X = res_het_X$Psi[, 1],   Proxy_IV = res_proxy$Psi[, 1],   Proxy_IV_rec = res_proxy_rec$Psi[, 1] ), 2)  knitr::kable(   tab_1,   col.names = c(     "True", "HET-IV without controls", "HET-IV with controls",     "Proxy-IV with controls", "Proxy-IV with recursive restriction"   ),   caption = paste(     "Impact matrix estimates ($\\Psi$) for shock 1 across different",     "specifications"   ) ) tab_2 <- round(data.frame(   True = PsiE[, 2],   HET_IV = res_het$Psi[, 2],   HET_IV_X = res_het_X$Psi[, 2],   Proxy_IV = res_proxy$Psi[, 2],   Proxy_IV_rec = res_proxy_rec$Psi[, 2] ), 2)  knitr::kable(   tab_2,   col.names = c(     "True", "HET-IV without controls", "HET-IV with controls",     "Proxy-IV with controls", "Proxy-IV with recursive restriction"   ),   caption = paste(     "Impact matrix estimates ($\\Psi$) for shock 2 across different",     "specifications"   ) )

title: Estimating Dynamic Causal Effects with hetiv
text: can assess accuracy estimates, dynamic causal effects, using plotirf(). function plots IRFs shaded confidence bands one estimation approach. confidence levels can chosen freely. , show 90%, 95%, 99% confidence intervals HET-IV estimates controls.
code: var_labels <- paste0("Variable ", 1:N)  plots_het_X <- plotirf(   IRFest = res_het_X$irf,   IRFse  = res_het_X$se,   HTick  = 5,   Labels = var_labels,   ci     = c(0.90, 0.95, 0.99) )  for (j in seq_len(E)) {   idx <- ((j - 1) * N + 1):(j * N)   panel <- cowplot::plot_grid(plotlist = plots_het_X[idx], ncol = 2)   title <- cowplot::ggdraw() +     cowplot::draw_label(       paste0("HET-IV with controls — Shock ", j, " (90%, 95%, 99% CI)"),       size = 11     )   print(cowplot::plot_grid(title, panel, ncol = 1, rel_heights = c(0.05, 1))) }

title: Estimating Dynamic Causal Effects with hetiv
text: can compare estimates true impulse responses. plot2irf() overlays two sets IRFs. compare HET-IV estimate (blue) theoretical IRF computed known VAR parameters (red). function computeirf() computes theoretical IRF. standard errors true IRF set zero, confidence bands plotted true IRF.
code: irf_true <- computeirf(PsiE, Phi, H, cum = FALSE) irf_true_se <- array(0, dim = dim(irf_true), dimnames = dimnames(irf_true))  plots_vs_true <- plot2irf(   IRF1   = res_het_X$irf,   IRF1se = res_het_X$se,   IRF2   = irf_true,   IRF2se = irf_true_se,   HTick  = 5,   Labels = var_labels,   ci     = 0.90 )  for (j in seq_len(E)) {   idx <- ((j - 1) * N + 1):(j * N)   panel <- cowplot::plot_grid(plotlist = plots_vs_true[idx], ncol = 2)   title <- cowplot::ggdraw() +     cowplot::draw_label(       paste0("HET-IV with controls (blue) vs True (red) — Shock ", j, " (90% CI)"),       size = 11     )   print(cowplot::plot_grid(title, panel, ncol = 1, rel_heights = c(0.05, 1))) }

title: Estimating Dynamic Causal Effects with hetiv
text: can also compare accuracy estimates using correct misspecified models.  see confidence intervals wider using misspecified model (red). especially true third fourth variables affected deterministic weekday pattern. misspecified model account pattern, leads uncertainty biased estimates. intervals also somewhat wider variables 1 2 fail control lagged dependent variables.
code: plots_vs_true <- plot2irf(   IRF1   = res_het_X$irf,   IRF1se = res_het_X$se,   IRF2   = res_het$irf,   IRF2se = res_het$se,   HTick  = 5,   Labels = var_labels,   ci     = 0.90 )  for (j in seq_len(E)) {   idx <- ((j - 1) * N + 1):(j * N)   panel <- cowplot::plot_grid(plotlist = plots_vs_true[idx], ncol = 2)   title <- cowplot::ggdraw() +     cowplot::draw_label(       paste0("HET-IV with controls (blue) vs without controls (red) — Shock ", j, " (90% CI)"),       size = 11     )   print(cowplot::plot_grid(title, panel, ncol = 1, rel_heights = c(0.05, 1))) }

title: Estimating Dynamic Causal Effects with hetiv
text: Next, compare HET-IV estimates Proxy-IV estimates. obtain relatively similar point estimates across two approaches.
code: plots_vs_proxy <- plot2irf(   IRF1   = res_het_X$irf,   IRF1se = res_het_X$se,   IRF2   = res_proxy$irf,   IRF2se = res_proxy$se,   HTick  = 5,   Labels = var_labels,   ci     = 0.90 )  for (j in seq_len(E)) {   idx <- ((j - 1) * N + 1):(j * N)   panel <- cowplot::plot_grid(plotlist = plots_vs_proxy[idx], ncol = 2)   title <- cowplot::ggdraw() +     cowplot::draw_label(       paste0("HET-IV (blue) vs Proxy-IV (red) — Shock ", j, " (90% CI)"),       size = 11     )   print(cowplot::plot_grid(title, panel, ncol = 1, rel_heights = c(0.05, 1))) }

title: Estimating Dynamic Causal Effects with hetiv
text: kfpredict() recovers structural shocks reduced-form residuals via Kalman filter prediction. requires estimated impact matrix Psi, residual covariance matrices event control days (Sig, SigR), residuals et. assess accuracy predictions comparing true event shocks underlying model. Correlation predicted shocks true shocks correlation true shocks proxy slightly lower one due attenuation bias (see Burri Kaufmann, 2026a), depends variance noise term affecting proxy. correlation prediction also relatively low use misspecified model. However, use correct model, correlation close unity Shock 1 0.94 Shock 2. Proxy-IV controls yields high correlation well, least Shock 1. Note specific values depend exact nature simulated data.
code: shocks_het <- kfpredict(   Sig = res_het$Sig, SigR = res_het$SigR,   Psi = res_het$Psi, et = res_het$et )  shocks_het_X <- kfpredict(   Sig = res_het_X$Sig, SigR = res_het_X$SigR,   Psi = res_het_X$Psi, et = res_het_X$et )  shocks_proxy <- kfpredict(   Sig = res_proxy$Sig, SigR = res_proxy$SigR,   Psi = res_proxy$Psi, et = res_proxy$et )  shocks_proxy_rec <- kfpredict(   Sig = res_proxy_rec$Sig, SigR = res_proxy_rec$SigR,   Psi = res_proxy_rec$Psi, et = res_proxy_rec$et ) true_shocks <- sim$eE  cor_df1 <- data.frame(   True = true_shocks[, 1],   Proxy = e_proxy[, 1],   HET_IV = shocks_het[, 1],   HET_IV_X = shocks_het_X[, 1],   Proxy_IV_X = shocks_proxy[, 1],   Proxy_IV_Rec = shocks_proxy_rec[, 1] )  cor_df2 <- data.frame(   True = true_shocks[, 2],   Proxy = e_proxy[, 2],   HET_IV = shocks_het[, 2],   HET_IV_X = shocks_het_X[, 2],   Proxy_IV_X = shocks_proxy[, 2],   Proxy_IV_Rec = shocks_proxy_rec[, 2] )  tab_all <- data.frame(   rbind(     round(cor(cor_df1, use = "complete.obs"), 2)[1, ],     round(cor(cor_df2, use = "complete.obs"), 2)[1, ]   ) ) rownames(tab_all) <- c("Shock 1", "Shock 2")  knitr::kable(   tab_all,   col.names = c(     "True", "Proxy", "HET-IV without controls", "HET-IV with controls",     "Proxy-IV with controls", "Proxy-IV with recursive restriction"   ),   row.names = TRUE,   caption = "Correlation of predicted shocks with true shocks" )

title: Estimating Dynamic Causal Effects with hetiv
text: gweakivtest() implements generalised minimum eigenvalue test Lewis Mertens (2025), robust heteroskedasticity autocorrelation applicable multiple endogenous regressors multiple instruments. Note classical Stock-Yogo (2005) test applicable homoskedastic case. hetiv() proxyiv() return WeakData object details = TRUE. data frame contains data expected gweakivtest(). code extracts relevant columns WeakData data frame runs weak instrument test four specifications. illustration, use heteroskedasticity-robust version (EHW). HAR (Newey West) version, use option NW. default, function uses bias tolerance 10% significance level 5%. Weak instrument test results (Lewis-Mertens generalised minimum eigenvalue test) specification without controls affected weak instrument problem. correctly specified model passes weak instrument tests. Proxy-IV fails test. However, note depends degree noise used construct proxy suggest hetiv() generally superior proxyiv(). Interestingly, least HET-IV, critical values much higher common rule thumb Stock Yogo (2005) test higher HAR test univariate case Montiel Olea Pflueger (2013) Lewis (2022), around 23.
code: # Helper: extract y, Y, X, Z from WeakData and run gweakivtest run_weaktest <- function(weakdata, E) {   # y: outcome variable E+1 (not used as endogenous regressor)   y <- weakdata[, paste0("y", E + 1)]   # Y: first E outcome variables (endogenous regressors)   Y <- weakdata[, paste0("y", 1:E)]   # Z: the E instruments   Z <- weakdata[, paste0("Z", 1:E), ]   # X: lagged ("o*"), deterministic ("x*"), and indicator ("i*") controls.   # gweakivtest() adds a constant term if one is missing.   ctrl <- startsWith(colnames(weakdata), "o") |     startsWith(colnames(weakdata), "x") |     startsWith(colnames(weakdata), "i")   X <- if (any(ctrl)) {     weakdata[, ctrl, drop = FALSE]   } else {     matrix(numeric(0), nrow(weakdata), 0)   }    gweakivtest(y, Y, X, Z, cov_type = "EHW") }  specs <- list(   "HET-IV, no controls" = res_het$WeakData,   "HET-IV, with controls" = res_het_X$WeakData,   "Proxy-IV, with controls" = res_proxy$WeakData,   "Proxy-IV, with controls + recursive restriction" = res_proxy_rec$WeakData )  wt_results <- lapply(specs, run_weaktest, E = E) tab <- do.call(rbind, lapply(names(wt_results), function(nm) {   r <- wt_results[[nm]]   data.frame(     Specification = nm,     Statistic = round(r$gmin_generalized, 2),     LM_CV = round(r$gmin_generalized_critical_value, 2),     Strong = ifelse(r$gmin_generalized > r$gmin_generalized_critical_value,       "Yes", "No"     ),     stringsAsFactors = FALSE   ) }))  knitr::kable(   tab,   col.names = c(     "Specification", "Statistic", "LM critical value",     "Strong instruments?"   ),   caption = "Weak instrument test results (Lewis-Mertens generalised minimum eigenvalue test)" )

title: hetiv
text: R package measuring identifying structural shocks using heteroskedasticity- proxy-based instrumental variable (IV) methods daily financial market data. Authors: Daniel Kaufmann, Marc Burri, Valentin Grob Comments, bug reports, feature requests welcome GitHub issue tracker.

title: hetiv
text: Full documentation worked example available package website: https://dankaufmann.github.io/hetiv/ introductory vignette – covering data simulation, HET-IV, Proxy-IV, IRF plots, shock extraction, weak instrument testing – : https://dankaufmann.github.io/hetiv/articles/hetiv-introduction.html Replication files Burri Kaufmann (2026b), providing real-world example, available : https://github.com/dankaufmann/bk_2026_eclet_replication/ gweakivtest() direct port Matlab files Lewis Mertens (2025) available https://karelmertens.com/research/.

title: hetiv
text: can install development version GitHub:
code: # install.packages("remotes") remotes::install_github("dankaufmann/hetiv")

title: hetiv
code: library(hetiv)  set.seed(1) N <- 2 E <- 1 Phi <- array(0, dim = c(N, N, 1)) Phi[, , 1] <- diag(c(0.4, 0.2)) sim <- simulatedata(   Phi = Phi, SigE = 2, PsiE = matrix(c(1, 0.5), N, 1),   PsiR = diag(N), Nobs = 100, Nbin = 20, N = N, R = N, E = E,   Nevn = 5, P = 1, eDist = 0, seed = 1 )  y <- sim$y O <- y Ind <- as.integer(sim$IndE[, 1])  res <- hetiv(y = y, O = O, Ind = Ind, P = 1, H = 20, E = 1, norm = 1, details = TRUE)  # With a linear time trend X <- matrix(seq_len(nrow(y)), ncol = 1) res <- hetiv(y = y, O = O, X = X, Ind = Ind, P = 1, H = 20, E = 1, norm = 1, details = TRUE)

title: hetiv
code: # Z: T x E matrix of external instruments (proxies) Z <- matrix(sim$eE[, 1] + rnorm(nrow(y)), ncol = 1) Z[Ind == 0, ] <- 0  res <- proxyiv(y = y, O = O, Z = Z, Ind = Ind, P = 1, H = 20, E = 1, norm = 1, details = TRUE)  # With recursive zero restrictions across shock dimensions res <- proxyiv(y = y, O = O, Z = Z, Ind = Ind, P = 1, H = 20, E = 2,                norm = 1, recursive = TRUE, details = TRUE)

title: hetiv
code: plots <- plotirf(IRFest = res$irf, IRFse = res$se,                  HTick = 5, Labels = c("Var 1", "Var 2")) cowplot::plot_grid(plotlist = plots)

title: hetiv
code: # y: T x 1 outcome variable # Y: T x N matrix of endogenous regressors # X: T x Nx matrix of exogenous controls # Z: T x K matrix of instruments (requires K >= N)  wt <- gweakivtest(y = y, Y = Y, X = X, Z = Z)  # Inspect test statistics and critical values wt$gmin_generalized                            # generalised min-eigenvalue statistic wt$gmin_generalized_critical_value             # Lewis-Mertens sharp critical value (Stiefel) wt$gmin_generalized_critical_value_simplified  # conservative closed-form bound  # With Newey-West HAR standard errors and custom bias tolerance wt <- gweakivtest(y = y, Y = Y, X = X, Z = Z, cov_type = "NW", tau = 0.10)  # hetiv() and proxyiv() return WeakData when details = TRUE, # which contains the outcome and instrument columns needed for gweakivtest(). Note that the E endogenous variables have to be ordered first res     <- hetiv(y = y, O = O, Ind = Ind, P = 1, H = 20, E = 1, details = TRUE) weakdata <- res$WeakData  # y: outcome variable E + 1 (not used as endogenous regressor) y_w <- weakdata[, paste0("y", E + 1)] # Y: first E outcome variables (endogenous regressors) Y_w <- weakdata[, paste0("y", 1:E)] # Z: the E instruments Z_w <- weakdata[, paste0("Z", 1:E), drop = FALSE] # X: lagged ("o*"), deterministic ("x*"), and indicator ("i*") controls. # gweakivtest() adds a constant term if one is missing. ctrl <- startsWith(colnames(weakdata), "o") |   startsWith(colnames(weakdata), "x") |   startsWith(colnames(weakdata), "i") X_w <- if (any(ctrl)) weakdata[, ctrl, drop = FALSE] else matrix(numeric(0), nrow(weakdata), 0)  wt <- gweakivtest(y = y_w, Y = Y_w, X = X_w, Z = Z_w)

title: hetiv
code: # Use estimates from hetiv() or proxyiv() to predict the unobserved underlying # shocks with the Kalman filter. shocks <- kfpredict(Sig = res$Sig, SigR = res$SigR,                     Psi = res$Psi, et = res$et)

title: hetiv
code: sim <- simulatedata(Phi = Phi, SigE = 4, PsiE = PsiE, PsiR = PsiR,                     Nobs = 500, Nbin = 100, N = 2, R = 2, E = 1,                     Nevn = 5, P = 1, eDist = 0, seed = 42)

title: hetiv

title: Compute impulse response functions for a VAR(P) — computeirf

title: Linearly interpolate gaps in a data frame — filllinear
code: filllinear(DF, gap)

title: Linearly interpolate gaps in a data frame — filllinear

code: firstdiff(TS)

title: Generalised weak instrument test — gweakivtest
text: Tests weak instruments multiple endogenous regressors using generalised minimum eigenvalue statistic Lewis Mertens (2025). test robust heteroskedasticity autocorrelation nests classical Stock-Yogo (2005) test special case. function direct port Matlab codes Lewis Mertens (2025)

title: Generalised weak instrument test — gweakivtest
code: gweakivtest(   y,   Y,   X,   Z,   cov_type = "EHW",   alfa = 0.05,   tau = 0.1,   points = 1000L,   target = "beta",   crit = "abs",   seed = 12345L )

title: Generalised weak instrument test — gweakivtest
text: y Regressand (T x 1 numeric vector matrix). Y Endogenous regressors (T x N numeric matrix). X Exogenous regressors (T x Nx numeric matrix). constant column added automatically one absent X zero columns. Z Instruments (T x K numeric matrix). Requires K >= N. cov_type HAR covariance estimator: "EHW" (Eicker-Huber-White, default) "NW" (Newey-West Lazarus et al. (2018) bandwidth). alfa Nominal significance level (default 0.05). tau Bias tolerance: maximum acceptable relative (absolute, see crit) bias 2SLS estimator (default 0.10). points Number random starting points Stiefel manifold optimisation used compute sharp critical value K > N + 1 (default 1000). target Either "beta" (default) test full coefficient vector, positive integer j <= N target single coefficient beta_j. crit Bias criterion: "abs" (absolute bias, default) "rel" (relative bias). "abs" requires error covariance matrix; "rel" . seed Integer seed used random Stiefel starting points computing sharp critical value. default fixed reproducible critical values caller's RNG state restored afterwards. Use NA leave RNG unmanaged.

title: Generalised weak instrument test — gweakivtest
text: named list following elements: nobs Number complete observations used. beta_2SLS 2SLS point estimate(s). target Description targeted parameter. criterion Bias criterion used ("abs" "rel"). gmin_generalized Generalised minimum eigenvalue test statistic (Lewis-Mertens). gmin_generalized_critical_value Sharp critical value via Stiefel optimisation. gmin_generalized_critical_value_simplified Conservative simplified critical value (closed-form bound). stock_yogo_test_statistic Stock-Yogo test statistic Nagar approximation. stock_yogo_critical_value_nagar Stock-Yogo critical value Nagar approximation.

title: Generalised weak instrument test — gweakivtest
text: Lazarus, E., Lewis, D. J., Stock, J. H. Watson, M. W. (2018). HAR inference: recommendations practice. Journal Business & Economic Statistics, 36(4), 541-559. Lewis, D. J. Mertens, K. (2025). robust test weak instruments 2SLS multiple endogenous regressors. Review Economic Studies, DOI: 10.1093/restud/rdaf103. Stock, J. H. Yogo, M. (2005). Testing weak instruments linear IV regression. D. W. K. Andrews J. H. Stock (Eds.), Identification inference econometric models: essays honor Thomas Rothenberg, pp. 80-108. Cambridge University Press.

title: Generalised weak instrument test — gweakivtest
code: set.seed(1) n <- 80 Z <- matrix(rnorm(n), ncol = 1) X <- matrix(1, n, 1) Y <- 0.8 * Z + rnorm(n) y <- 1.5 * Y + rnorm(n) gweakivtest(y = y, Y = Y, X = X, Z = Z, points = 1) #> $nobs #> [1] 80 #>  #> $beta_2SLS #> [1] 1.885098 #>  #> $target #> [1] "beta vector" #>  #> $criterion #> [1] "abs" #>  #> $gmin_generalized #> [1] 18.31699 #>  #> $gmin_generalized_critical_value #> [1] 14.39938 #>  #> $gmin_generalized_critical_value_simplified #> [1] 14.39938 #>  #> $stock_yogo_test_statistic #> [1] 20.28379 #>  #> $stock_yogo_critical_value_nagar #> [1] NaN #>

what: hetiv: Heteroskedasticity and Proxy-Based IV Methods — hetiv-package
title: hetiv: Heteroskedasticity and Proxy-Based IV Methods — hetiv-package
text: Tools identifying structural shocks using heteroskedasticity- proxy-based instrumental variable (IV) methods event-study settings. Supports local projection impulse response estimation Jorda (2005) doi:10.1257/0002828053828518 , generalised weak instrument testing Lewis Mertens (2025) doi:10.1093/restud/rdaf103 , Kalman-filter shock extraction Burri Kaufmann (2026) doi:10.1016/j.econlet.2026.113091 .

title: hetiv: Heteroskedasticity and Proxy-Based IV Methods — hetiv-package
text: Maintainer: Daniel Kaufmann info@dankaufmann.com [copyright holder] Authors: Marc Burri Valentin Grob

what: Estimate impulse responses via heteroskedasticity-based IV local projections — hetiv
title: Estimate impulse responses via heteroskedasticity-based IV local projections — hetiv
text: Estimates impulse response functions (IRFs) using recursive heteroskedasticity-IV identification (Rigobon, 2003; Rigobon Sack, 2004; Lewis, 2022; Burri Kaufmann, 2026a, 2026b) combined local projections (Jorda, 2005). Identification exploits difference variance policy event days control days construct instruments endogenous variables.

title: Estimate impulse responses via heteroskedasticity-based IV local projections — hetiv
code: hetiv(   y,   O,   X = NULL,   Ind,   P,   H,   E = 1,   norm = 1,   interact = FALSE,   cum = FALSE,   Hstep = 1,   cov_type = "HC0",   details = FALSE )

title: Estimate impulse responses via heteroskedasticity-based IV local projections — hetiv
text: y Numeric matrix stationary outcome variables (T x N). effect first variable dimension normalized unity horizon 0. variables also used construct heteroskedasticity-based instruments recursive ordering identify multiple dimensions. O Numeric matrix information set variables (T x M). May identical y. Included lags 1 P. X Numeric matrix deterministic variables (T x K). example, time trend, seasonal dummies deterministic controls. Included (lags). constant included default. Ind Integer vector length T, event indicator: 0 Control day (event) 1 Policy day (event) 2 Contaminated control day (excluded estimation) P Integer. Maximum lag order information set. Set 0 lags (regression deterministic terms ). H Integer. Maximum horizon (periods) IRFs estimated. E Integer. Number shock dimensions identify via recursive ordering. norm Numeric scalar. Normalize impact response first variable specific value. Set 1 standard unit-effect normalization. interact Logical. TRUE, lagged information set variables interacted event/non-event dummies. cum Logical vector length N. variable y, whether report cumulative impulse response instead level response. one provided, applied impulse responses. Hstep Integer. Step size horizons. default 1 estimates horizons 0 H - 1. Values greater 1 estimate selected horizons. cov_type Covariance estimator local-projection standard errors: "HC0" (default) heteroskedasticity-robust standard errors "NW" Newey-West HAC standard errors. "HC0" default Montiel Olea et al. (2025) show heteroskedasticity-robust standard errors suffice local-projection impulse responses weak conditions, even though multi-step forecast errors typically serially correlated. "NW" remains available optional HAC robustness check. details Logical. TRUE, code saves detailed IV results, slightly slower. set FALSE, returns impulse response standard error (e.g. bootstrap)

title: Estimate impulse responses via heteroskedasticity-based IV local projections — hetiv
text: named list following elements: irf Array (H x N x E) estimated impulse responses. se Array (H x N x E) local-projection standard errors. IVRes List ivreg model objects, one per horizon, variable, shock dimension. Obs Data frame observation counts: Tp (policy days), Tc (control days), (contaminated days), Tt (total used). Method Character string "Heteroscedasticity-IV". et Data frame OLS residuals event days (used covariance estimation shock extraction). Sig Covariance matrix residuals event days (used shock extraction). SigR Covariance matrix residuals control days, NA unavailable (used shock extraction). Psi Impact matrix (N x E), equal irf[1, , ]. package's indexing convention HSeries starts 1, first LP uses lead(y, 0) (contemporaneous value) labelled horizon 0; irf[1, , ] therefore always impact response. WeakData Data frame endogenous variables instruments Lewis-Mertens (2025) weak instrument test.

title: Estimate impulse responses via heteroskedasticity-based IV local projections — hetiv
text: E > 1, identification recursive order-dependent: column order y defines shock ordering normalization variable shock dimension.

title: Estimate impulse responses via heteroskedasticity-based IV local projections — hetiv
text: Burri, M. D. Kaufmann (2026a). Measuring monetary policy shocks. Economics Letters. doi:10.1016/j.econlet.2026.113091 Burri, M. D. Kaufmann (2026b). Multiple monetary policy shocks daily data: heteroskedasticity IV approach. IRENE Working Papers 26-06, IRENE Institute Economic Research, University Neuchatel. Jorda, O. (2005). Estimation inference impulse responses local projections. American Economic Review, 95(1), 161-182. Lewis, D. J. (2022). Robust inference models identified via heteroskedasticity. Review Economics Statistics, 104(3), 510-524. Lewis, D. J. Mertens, K. (2025). robust test weak instruments 2SLS multiple endogenous regressors. Review Economic Studies, DOI: 10.1093/restud/rdaf103 Montiel Olea, J. L., M. Plagborg-Moller, E. Qian, C. K. Wolf (2025). Local projections VARs? primer macroeconomists. NBER Working Paper . 33871. Rigobon, R. (2003). Identification heteroskedasticity. Review Economics Statistics, 85(4), 777-792. Rigobon, R. Sack, B. (2004). impact monetary policy asset prices. Journal Monetary Economics, 51(8), 1553-1575.

title: Estimate impulse responses via heteroskedasticity-based IV local projections — hetiv
code: set.seed(1) y <- matrix(rnorm(80), ncol = 2) Ind <- rep(0L, nrow(y)) Ind[seq(5, nrow(y), by = 5)] <- 1L res <- hetiv(y = y, O = y, Ind = Ind, P = 1, H = 3, details = TRUE) dim(res$irf) #> [1] 3 2 1

what: Extract monetary policy shocks via Kalman filter — kfpredict
title: Extract monetary policy shocks via Kalman filter — kfpredict
text: Extracts structural shocks reduced-form residuals using Kalman filter formula. Given impact matrix Psi covariance matrices residuals event control days, function recovers latent shocks observation. Optionally rescales shocks unit variance.

title: Extract monetary policy shocks via Kalman filter — kfpredict
code: kfpredict(Sig, SigR, Psi, et, tol = sqrt(.Machine$double.eps), scale = TRUE)

title: Extract monetary policy shocks via Kalman filter — kfpredict
text: Sig Numeric matrix (N x N). Covariance matrix reduced-form residuals policy event days (e.g. et hetiv()). SigR Numeric matrix (N x N). Covariance matrix reduced-form residuals control (non-event) days. Used back implied shock variances scale = TRUE. Psi Numeric matrix (N x E). Impact matrix, .e. contemporaneous responses N variables E structural shocks (e.g. Psi hetiv()). et Numeric matrix data frame (T x N). Reduced-form residuals event days (e.g. et hetiv()). Rows correspond time periods, columns variables. tol Numeric scalar. Tolerance generalized inverse (MASS::ginv()). Clipped sqrt(.Machine$double.eps). scale Logical. TRUE (default), shocks rescaled unit variance using implied shock variances recovered Sig SigR. scaling assumes structural shock variances diagonal, Sig - SigR = sum_i sigma_i Psi_i Psi_i'. FALSE, raw Kalman filter projection returned.

title: Extract monetary policy shocks via Kalman filter — kfpredict
text: numeric matrix (T x E) extracted structural shocks, number rows et one column per shock dimension.

title: Extract monetary policy shocks via Kalman filter — kfpredict
text: Burri, M. Kaufmann, D. (2026). Measuring monetary policy shocks. Economics Letters. doi:10.1016/j.econlet.2026.113091

title: Extract monetary policy shocks via Kalman filter — kfpredict

what: Plot and compare impulse responses from two estimation approaches — plot2irf
title: Plot and compare impulse responses from two estimation approaches — plot2irf
text: Produces panel IRF plots comparing two sets impulse responses. panel shows point estimates confidence bands level ci approaches. Returns list ggplot objects (one per variable-shock combination) can arranged e.g. cowplot::plot_grid().

title: Plot and compare impulse responses from two estimation approaches — plot2irf
code: plot2irf(IRF1, IRF1se, IRF2, IRF2se, HTick, Labels, ci = 0.9)

title: Plot and compare impulse responses from two estimation approaches — plot2irf
text: IRF1 Array (H x N x E) impulse responses first approach. Dimnames first dimension must horizon labels (numeric). IRF1se Array (H x N x E) standard errors first approach (set 0 suppress confidence intervals). IRF2 Array (H x N x E) impulse responses second approach. IRF2se Array (H x N x E) standard errors second approach (set 0 suppress confidence intervals). HTick Integer. Step size x-axis tick marks. Labels Character vector length N. Variable names used panel titles. ci Numeric scalar (0, 1). Confidence level shaded bands. Defaults 0.90 (90% CI).

title: Plot and compare impulse responses from two estimation approaches — plot2irf
text: list ggplot objects, one per variable-shock combination, ordered shock (outer loop) variable (inner loop).

title: Plot and compare impulse responses from two estimation approaches — plot2irf
code: irf1 <- array(c(1, 0.5, 0.2, 0.1), dim = c(4, 1, 1)) dimnames(irf1)[[1]] <- 0:3 irf2 <- irf1 * 0.8 se <- array(0.1, dim = dim(irf1), dimnames = dimnames(irf1)) plot2irf(irf1, se, irf2, se * 0, HTick = 1, Labels = "Output") #> [[1]]  #>

what: Plot impulse responses from a single estimation approach — plotirf
title: Plot impulse responses from a single estimation approach — plotirf
text: Produces panel IRF plots single set impulse responses, optional confidence bands. Returns list ggplot objects (one per variable-shock combination) can arranged e.g. cowplot::plot_grid().

title: Plot impulse responses from a single estimation approach — plotirf
code: plotirf(IRFest, IRFse = NULL, HTick, Labels, ci = c(0.9, 0.95))

title: Plot impulse responses from a single estimation approach — plotirf
text: IRFest Array (H x N x E) impulse response point estimates. Dimnames first dimension must horizon labels (numeric). IRFse Array (H x N x E) standard errors, NULL suppress confidence bands. HTick Integer. Step size x-axis tick marks. Labels Character vector length N. Variable names used panel titles. ci Numeric vector confidence levels (0, 1). One shaded band drawn per level, wider bands rendered lower opacity. Defaults c(0.90, 0.95).

title: Plot impulse responses from a single estimation approach — plotirf
text: list ggplot objects, one per variable-shock combination, ordered shock (outer loop) variable (inner loop).

title: Plot impulse responses from a single estimation approach — plotirf
code: irf <- array(c(1, 0.5, 0.2, 0.1), dim = c(4, 1, 1)) dimnames(irf)[[1]] <- 0:3 se <- array(0.1, dim = dim(irf), dimnames = dimnames(irf)) plotirf(irf, se, HTick = 1, Labels = "Output") #> [[1]]  #>

title: Plot p-values across horizons — plotpval
text: Produces panel p-value plots across IRF horizons, optional horizontal reference lines user-supplied significance levels. Returns list ggplot objects (one per variable-shock combination) can arranged e.g. cowplot::plot_grid().

title: Plot p-values across horizons — plotpval
code: plotpval(pvals, HTick, Labels, sigLevels = c(0.05, 0.1))

title: Plot p-values across horizons — plotpval
text: pvals Array (H x N x E) p-values. Dimnames first dimension must horizon labels (numeric). HTick Integer. Step size x-axis tick marks. Labels Character vector length N. Variable names used panel titles. sigLevels Numeric vector significance levels draw horizontal reference lines. first value drawn solid line, second dashed, values dotted. Defaults c(0.05, 0.10).

title: Plot p-values across horizons — plotpval
text: list ggplot objects, one per variable-shock combination, ordered shock (outer loop) variable (inner loop).

title: Plot p-values across horizons — plotpval
code: pvals <- array(c(0.2, 0.08, 0.04), dim = c(3, 1, 1)) dimnames(pvals)[[1]] <- 0:2 plotpval(pvals, HTick = 1, Labels = "Output") #> [[1]]  #>

what: Estimate impulse responses via proxy-IV local projections — proxyiv
title: Estimate impulse responses via proxy-IV local projections — proxyiv
text: Estimates impulse response functions (IRFs) using user-provided external instruments (proxies) combined local projections (Jorda, 2005). proxy variables serve directly instruments endogenous shock variables. Optionally imposes recursive zero restrictions across shock dimensions supports deterministic controls following conventions hetiv().

title: Estimate impulse responses via proxy-IV local projections — proxyiv
code: proxyiv(   y,   O,   Z,   X = NULL,   Ind,   P,   H,   E = 1,   norm = 1,   cum = FALSE,   Hstep = 1,   cov_type = "HC0",   recursive = FALSE,   details = FALSE )

title: Estimate impulse responses via proxy-IV local projections — proxyiv
text: y Numeric matrix stationary outcome variables (T x N). effect first variable dimension normalized norm horizon 0. variables also used endogenous regressors instrumented columns Z. O Numeric matrix information set variables (T x M). May identical y. Included lags 1 P. Z Numeric matrix external instruments (T x E). Column e used proxy shock dimension e. Missing values control days (Ind == 0) treated contaminated excluded estimation. Missing values policy days (Ind == 1) remain labelled events residual-based shock prediction, necessarily dropped IV regressions use missing proxy. X Numeric matrix deterministic variables (T x K), NULL (default). May include constant, time trend, seasonal dummies. Included -(lags). Ind Integer vector length T, event indicator: 0 Control day (event) 1 Policy day (event) 2 Contaminated control day (excluded estimation) P Integer. Maximum lag order information set. Set 0 lags (regression constant ). H Integer. Maximum horizon (periods) IRFs estimated. E Integer. Number shock dimensions identify. Default 1. norm Numeric scalar. Normalize impact response first variable value. Set 1 standard unit-effect normalization. cum Logical scalar vector length N. TRUE variable , cumulative IRF reported. single value recycled variables. Default FALSE. Hstep Integer. Step size horizons. default 1 estimates horizons 0 H - 1. Values greater 1 estimate selected horizons. Default 1. cov_type Covariance estimator local-projection standard errors: "HC0" (default) heteroskedasticity-robust standard errors "NW" Newey-West HAC standard errors. "HC0" default Montiel Olea et al. (2025) show heteroskedasticity-robust standard errors suffice local-projection impulse responses weak conditions, even though multi-step forecast errors typically serially correlated. "NW" remains available optional HAC robustness check. recursive Logical. TRUE, imposes recursive zero restrictions across shock dimensions: shock e > 1, variables instruments dimensions 1, ..., e-1 added controls. Default FALSE. details Logical. TRUE, returns detailed results including IV model objects, OLS residuals, covariance matrices. FALSE (default), returns impulse responses standard errors (faster; use bootstrap).

title: Estimate impulse responses via proxy-IV local projections — proxyiv
text: named list. Always contains: irf Array (H x N x E) estimated impulse responses. se Array (H x N x E) local-projection standard errors. Method Character string "Proxy-IV". details = TRUE, additionally contains: IVRes List ivreg model objects, one per horizon, variable, shock dimension. OLSRes List OLS model objects used residual-based covariance estimation, one per outcome variable. Obs Data frame observation counts: Tp (policy days), Tc (control days), (contaminated days), Tt (labelled event control days), Tiv (complete observations available IV regression impact horizon). et Data frame OLS residuals event days. Sig Covariance matrix residuals event days. SigR Covariance matrix residuals control days, NA unavailable. Psi Impact matrix (N x E), equal irf[1, , ]. package's indexing convention HSeries starts 1, first LP uses lead(y, 0) (contemporaneous value) labelled horizon 0; irf[1, , ] therefore always impact response. WeakData Data frame endogenous variables instruments Lewis-Mertens (2025) weak instrument test.

title: Estimate impulse responses via proxy-IV local projections — proxyiv
text: E > 1, identification can order-dependent: column order y defines endogenous shock variables normalizations, column order Z assigns proxies shock dimensions, recursive = TRUE imposes restrictions order.

title: Estimate impulse responses via proxy-IV local projections — proxyiv
text: Jorda, O. (2005). Estimation inference impulse responses local projections. American Economic Review, 95(1), 161-182. Lewis, D. J. Mertens, K. (2025). robust test weak instruments 2SLS multiple endogenous regressors. Review Economic Studies, DOI: 10.1093/restud/rdaf103 Mertens, K. Ravn, M. O. (2013). dynamic effects personal corporate income tax changes United States. American Economic Review, 103(4), 1212-1247. Montiel Olea, J. L., M. Plagborg-Moller, E. Qian, C. K. Wolf (2025). Local projections VARs? primer macroeconomists. NBER Working Paper . 33871. Stock, J. H. Watson, M. W. (2018). Identification estimation dynamic causal effects macroeconomics using external instruments. Economic Journal, 128(610), 917-948.

title: Estimate impulse responses via proxy-IV local projections — proxyiv
code: set.seed(1) y <- matrix(rnorm(80), ncol = 2) Ind <- rep(0L, nrow(y)) Ind[seq(5, nrow(y), by = 5)] <- 1L Z <- matrix(Ind * y[, 1] + rnorm(nrow(y)), ncol = 1) res <- proxyiv(   y = y, O = y, Z = Z, Ind = Ind, P = 1, H = 3,   details = TRUE ) dim(res$irf) #> [1] 3 2 1

what: Simulate VAR data with heteroskedastic shocks and given parameters — simulatedata
title: Simulate VAR data with heteroskedastic shocks and given parameters — simulatedata
text: Simulates data VAR(P) model two types structural shocks: regular shocks (always present) event shocks (occurring every Nevn periods). shock distribution can normal, Student-t, GARCH(1,1).

title: Simulate VAR data with heteroskedastic shocks and given parameters — simulatedata
code: simulatedata(Phi, SigE, PsiE, PsiR, Nobs, Nbin, N, R, E, Nevn, P, eDist, seed)

title: Simulate VAR data with heteroskedastic shocks and given parameters — simulatedata
text: Phi Array VAR coefficient matrices, dimension N x N x P. SigE Variance event shocks. applies E shocks. PsiE Impact matrix event shocks, dimension N x E. PsiR Impact matrix regular shocks, dimension N x R. Nobs Number observations retain burn-. returned data exactly Nobs rows. Nbin Number burn-observations. simulated initialise VAR discarded returning. N Number variables VAR. R Number regular shocks. E Number event shocks. GARCH shock distributions (eDist = c(alpha, beta)), E = 1 supported. Nevn Event frequency: event shock occurs every Nevn periods. Set 0 suppress event shocks entirely (heteroskedasticity). P VAR lag order. eDist Shock distribution. Use 0 standard normal; positive integer Student-t many degrees freedom; numeric vector c(alpha, beta) GARCH(1,1) ARCH effect alpha persistence beta. seed Integer seed passed set.seed() reproducibility. Use NA skip seeding.

title: Simulate VAR data with heteroskedastic shocks and given parameters — simulatedata
text: named list components: y Simulated VAR data, dimension Nobs x N (burn-discarded). IndE Event indicator matrix, dimension Nobs x 1. eR Simulated regular shocks, dimension Nobs x R. eE Simulated event shocks, dimension Nobs x E. e Composite structural shocks, dimension Nobs x N. Phi VAR coefficient array (returned unchanged). PsiE Event shock impact matrix (returned unchanged). PsiR Regular shock impact matrix (returned unchanged). SigE Event shock variance (returned unchanged).

title: Simulate VAR data with heteroskedastic shocks and given parameters — simulatedata
hetiv 1.0.0
text: Initial CRAN-ready release. Provides heteroskedasticity-based proxy-based instrumental variable local projection estimators event-study settings. Includes impulse response plotting helpers, weak instrument testing, Kalman-filter shock extraction, simulation utilities. Adds introductory vignette worked end--end example.
